summary: During World War II, my family and I were evacuated to the Manzanar Camp. Life there was difficult, but we managed to live through it with the help of neighbors and the community. I remember the camp as a place where many Japanese Americans were forced to stay, including us. Despite the hardships, I was grateful to survive with my large family.

I was born in Misaza-cho, Hiroshima, which is near Tottori. My early childhood there was simple; I attended elementary school for four years and enjoyed playing in the mountains and fields with friends. Life in Misasa was peaceful before I left for the United States. My family roots and early memories are deeply tied to this place.

I was born in Hiroshima in the twenty-second year of the Meiji period. Although I left Japan at a young age, Hiroshima was my home and the place where my family lived. I often thought about my mother and family there, especially when I returned to Japan in 1961. Hiroshima holds my early memories and family connections.

I was born in the twenty-second year of the Meiji period, which places my birth in the late 19th century. This era was a time of change in Japan, though my childhood memories focus more on family and school. The Meiji period marks the beginning of my life story before I eventually moved to the United States.

I came to the United States in the second year of the Taishō period, which was about sixty years ago from now. This period was when I left Japan to join my husband in America. The Taishō period marks an important transition in my life from Japan to the U.S.

I arrived in the United States in 1914 to join my husband, who had been here before me. Life in the U.S. was very hard, especially during the Great Depression when we struggled to make ends meet on our farm in Florin, California. Despite the challenges, I found community through church and the kindness of neighbors. The U.S. became my home, though I often missed Japan and worried about my family there.

When I arrived in San Francisco, I was taken to Angel Island for inspection. We stayed there for three or four days while undergoing physical exams and answering questions. It was a tense time, but eventually I was able to leave and join my husband in Florin. Angel Island was the gateway to my new life in America.

My husband and I lived in Florin, California, where we farmed grapes and strawberries on a 10-acre farm. We faced many struggles, especially during the Great Depression when prices dropped and we had to dig out grape trees. Our children helped a lot, but eventually we sold the farm before being moved to an assembly center during the war. Florin was where we built our family life in America.

The Great Depression was the hardest time in my life. In 1933, we couldn't sell our grapes for months and barely had enough to eat. A kind man named Mr. Yamada from Sacramento helped us by giving rice and food that kept us alive. Despite the poverty and hunger, none of our children got sick, and we survived with the support of our community and faith.

Sacramento was important to us because Mr. Yamada, who lived there, helped my family during the Depression by giving us rice and food. He and his wife also introduced us to Christianity and took us to church every Sunday. Sacramento was a place where we found kindness and spiritual support during very difficult times.

Christianity became a source of comfort and hope for me and my family after we were introduced to it by the Yamadas. Attending church and joining the ladies' association gave me joy during hard times. The faith helped me through struggles, including my husband's illness and the challenges of living in America as a Japanese immigrant.

A friend who was a member of the Salvation Army introduced me to Reverend Baba from Oxnard, who gave a sermon that deeply moved me. After receiving his blessing, I felt a spiritual transformation that helped me overcome despair. The Salvation Army played a key role in my spiritual journey and healing during difficult times.

Oxnard is where Reverend Baba, a Salvation Army minister, was based. I attended his sermon there after being invited by a friend. His blessing had a profound effect on me, helping me find peace and happiness during a very hard period in my life. Oxnard, through Reverend Baba, became a place connected to my spiritual renewal.

When I heard about the Pearl Harbor attack, I was worried about Japan and the consequences it would bring. My husband said Japan had done a terrible thing, but we felt powerless to change anything. The attack marked a turning point that eventually led to our evacuation and internment during the war.
